coredata.dc:title: Bright carbon dots as fluorescence sensing agents for bacteria and curcumin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Baig, Mirza Muhammad Fahad
Chen, Yu-Chie
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots) are fluorescent nanomaterials that possess good photostability and low toxicity. They have been used as sensing probes and bioimaging agents for a variety of biological species. Numerous methods are available to generate C-dots. Nevertheless, simple and straightforward synthesis methods must be explored for the synthesis of C-dots from inexpensive, natural sources. In this study, we developed a simple method to generate C-dots from inexpensive chicken egg whites through a one-step heating reaction. The size of the generated C-dots was 3.3±0.4nm, and the quantum yield of the C-dots was as high as ∼43%. The as-prepared C-dots can be used as multicolor labeling agents for bacteria such as Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. Furthermore, the generated C-dots can be used as Förster resonance energy transfer sensing probes for curcumin, which is an active ingredient of turmeric and medicinal pigment. The feasibility of using the C-dots as selective sensing probes to determine the amount of curcumin from complex turmeric powder and condensed turmeric tablets is also demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Bacteria
Curcumin
Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Using C-dots as FRET sensing agents for curcumin
Using C-dots as bacterial labeling agents
Determination of curcumin in turmeric products

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectrum (λex =300nm) of the generated C-dots (0.4mg/mL) prepared in deionized water. The inset in Panel A: optical images obtained under room lighting (Scale bar: 10nm) (the left vial) and UV lamp (λex =365nm) (the right vial). (B) UV absorption spectrum of the generated C-dots (0.4mg/mL) prepared in deionized water (Inset: HR-TEM image of the C-dots). (C) Fluorescence spectra of C-dots (0.1mg/mL) prepared in deionized water with excitation wavelengths changed from 300nm to 475nm.
(A) FT-IR and (B) Raman spectra of the as-prepared C-dots.

(A) XPS survey spectrum of the C-dots. (B) XPS C1s spectrum of the C-dots. (C) XPS N1s spectrum of the C-dots. (D) XPS O1s spectrum of the C-dots. All the spectra were calibrated using Na1s as internal standard (1072.5eV).

Fluorescence microscopic images (A–D) of C-dots only, (E–H) S. aureus only, (I–L) S. aureus labeled with C-dots, (M–P) E. coli only, (Q–T) E. coli labeled with C-dots under a bright field, λex =330–380nm, λex =450–490nm, and λex =510–560nm. The exposure time was 100ms. The scale bar is 10μm.
Spectral overlapping of the donor C-dots emission spectrum and curcumin absorption spectrum. Inset: structure of curcumin.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of C-dots (50μL, 50μgml−1) obtained by vortex-mixing with curcumin (50μL) at different concentrations prepared in a solvent of methanol and aqueous sodium acetate (pH 4.0, 0.2M) (1:1, v/v) for 30min. The concentrations of curcumin from top to bottom were 0, 0.78, 1.56, 3.13, 6.25, 12.50, 25.00, and 50.00μM. (B) The corresponding calibration curve obtained by plotting F0/F of C-dots at 395nm versus the concentration of curcumin. F0 and F stand for the fluorescence intensity at 395nm without and with the addition of curcumin, respectively. Three replicates were conducted for each data point.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the turmeric samples (40μL, 1mgmL−1) spiked with curcumin (10μL) at different concentrations (31.25, 62.50, 125, and 250μM) using C-dots (10μL, 0.4mgmL−1) as the sensing probes. (B) The corresponding calibration curve obtained by plotting F0/F versus the concentration of the spiked curcumin. F0 and F stand for fluorescence intensity at 395nm without and with addition of curcumin, respectively. Three replicates were conducted for each data point.

head.ce:title: Bright carbon dots as fluorescence sensing agents for bacteria and curcumin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, National Chiao Tung University, Hsinchu 300, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (C-dots) are fluorescent nanomaterials that possess good photostability and low toxicity. They have been used as sensing probes and bioimaging agents for a variety of biological species. Numerous methods are available to generate C-dots. Nevertheless, simple and straightforward synthesis methods must be explored for the synthesis of C-dots from inexpensive, natural sources. In this study, we developed a simple method to generate C-dots from inexpensive chicken egg whites through a one-step heating reaction. The size of the generated C-dots was 3.3±0.4nm, and the quantum yield of the C-dots was as high as ∼43%. The as-prepared C-dots can be used as multicolor labeling agents for bacteria such as Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli. Furthermore, the generated C-dots can be used as Förster resonance energy transfer sensing probes for curcumin, which is an active ingredient of turmeric and medicinal pigment. The feasibility of using the C-dots as selective sensing probes to determine the amount of curcumin from complex turmeric powder and condensed turmeric tablets is also demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-dots) have several unique features, including small sizes, ease-of-generation, low toxicity, and tunable fluorescence [1]. Thus, they have been widely applied in research fields such as bioimaging, sensing, optoelectronics, and photocatalysis [2–4]. C-dots can be generated from different carbon-rich organic sources [5]. Furthermore, numerous synthesis methods are available to generate C-dots. C-dots are generated through hydrothermal approaches [6], pyrolysis [7], microwave irradiation [5,8], laser ablation [9], chemical oxidation [10], electrochemical carbonization [11], and arc discharge [12]. However, hazardous chemicals [10,12], such as strong acids, and specific equipment, such as plasma generators [7], autoclaves [13], muffle furnaces [14], and pulsed lasers [4], are also required in some of these synthesis methods. Thus, green approaches [13,15–26] using natural sources as the starting materials in order to reduce the use of hazardous chemicals are desirable. Natural products, including fruits [16,22,24], chitosan [26], and chicken eggs [7,13,27], have been demonstrated to be suitable starting materials for the generation of C-dots.

Doping nitrogen and sulfur to C-dots can improve quantum yield (QY) [28–30]. Thus, strong acids or nitrogen/sulfur-containing reagents are commonly added to the reaction mixture to increase the doping amount of sulfur and nitrogen in the generated C-dots [26,31,32]. In addition, C-dots with long excitation/emission wavelengths are ideal for in vivo bioimaging. Increasing nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of C-dots and the particle size of C-dots can prolong excitation/emission wavelengths [33,34]. Starting materials with abundant nitrogen or sulfur-rich ingredients are ideal for generating C-dots. Inexpensive chicken eggs are a suitable candidate to be used as starting materials [7,13,27] because they are rich in carbon and nitrogen. C-dots can be generated from egg whites using rapid plasma treatment [7], hydrothermal methods [13], or alkaline hydrolysis approaches [27]. The QY of the C-dots generated from egg whites obtained through rapid plasma treatment was ∼6% [7]. C-dots with ∼61% QY were achieved when a hydrothermal approach was employed to heat egg whites at 220°C for 48h [13]. Although the QY of the generated C-dots obtained through hydrothermal approaches was high, the reaction time was quite long. The QY of the C-dots generated from egg whites through alkaline hydrolysis was only ∼16.8%, but the reaction time was ∼80h [27]. In addition, adding a strong acid and base was required when conducting the hydrolysis reaction [27]. Thus, producing C-dots from chicken egg whites with a high QY within a short reaction time without using hazardous reagents is anticipated.

For ease of observation, the labeling of pathogens with proper fluorescent dyes is often required [35]. Fluorescent C-dots have been used to label pathogenic bacteria, such as Escherichia coli [25,36] and Pseudomonas aeruginosa [37]. Most of the current methods rely on cell fixation with a long incubation time [25,37]. In addition, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), which requires a donor–acceptor pair of chromophores [38], has been applied to chemical and biochemical sensing [39]. Most existing FRET donors are either semiconductor quantum dots [15] or organic dyes [26]. Semiconductor quantum dots generally have high toxicity, whereas organic dyes are relatively unstable. Thus, FRET donors with high QY and high energy transfer efficiency are favorable. Given the high QY, good water solubility, and good biocompatibility, C-dots have been considered as donors in FRET for biosensing [40,41] and bioimaging [42]. C-dots can also be excited by light in the electromagnetic region from ultraviolet to blue. As such, their excitation light can be quite far from the excitation wavelength of the acceptors used in FRET [43]. Thus, C-dots have been demonstrated to be suitable FRET donors for several small molecules [44]. In this study, we propose a straightforward approach to generate bright C-dots with a desirable QY by a simple heating method. The feasibility of using the generated C-dots as labeling agents for bacterial cells and FRET sensors for curcumin is also demonstrated.

Pyrene and ammonium acetate were obtained from Riedel-de Haën (Seelze, Germany). Curcumin, n-hexane, and methanol were acquired from Acros (Morris Plains, NJ, USA), Tedia (Fairfield, OH, USA), and Macron (Center Valley, PA, USA), respectively. l-lysine, l-glutamine, l-histidine, l-cysteine, and sodium acetate were all obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Tryptic soy broth (TSB) and yeast extract were purchased from Becton-Dickinson (Franklin Lakes, NJ, USA). Agarose was obtained from Amresco Life Science (Solon, OH, USA). Staphylococcus aureus was provided by Prof. P.-J. Tsai (National Cheng Kung University, Taiwan). E. coli O157:H7 (BCRC 15376) was obtained from the Bioresource Collection Research Center (Hsinchu, Taiwan). Chicken eggs and turmeric powder (Sakhti brand, India) were purchased from a local supermarket. Condensed turmeric tablets (CROSS Corporation, Osaka, Japan) were purchased from a local pharmacy. Ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm−1), purified by a Merck Millipore system, was used in the experiments. Pall advance centrifugal devices were purchased from Pall Life Sciences (Ann Arbor, MI, USA).

Fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Horiba Jobin Yvon Flouromax-3 spectrofluorometer (Edison, NJ, USA). Lifetime measurement and Raman spectra were obtained using a Protrustech Uni-Probe spectrophotometer (Taipei, Taiwan) equipped with a pulsed laser (λ=405nm) and a continuous laser (λ=532nm). FluoFit software version 4.5 was used for data analysis. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) absorption spectra were acquired using a Perkin Elmer spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a PHI 5000 Versa Probe II X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS) (Edina, MN, USA) and a Varian Cary 50 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Melbourne, Australia), respectively. Zeta potential was determined by a Beckman Coulte Delsa Nano™ C (Fullerton, CA, USA). Bacterial images were recorded using a Nikon Eclipse 80i fluorescence microscope (Tokyo, Japan). Images of the generated C-dots were observed using a high-resolution JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) (Tokyo, Japan). ImageJ (1.48 v) software was used to estimate the particle size distribution of the C-dots from TEM images. Spectral overlapping was conducted with a UV–vis–IR spectral software, which is a freely available software package [45].

Egg whites were carefully isolated from fresh chicken eggs using a pipette. The isolated egg whites (∼250mg) were dissolved in deionized water (2mL) in a glass bottle. After vigorously stirring for ∼10min, the egg white liquid was directly heated to 200°C on a hot plate for 4h. Brown-black residues were generated on the bottom of the sample bottle. After cooling to room temperature, the residues were re-suspended in deionized water (2mL) under gentle stirring for 1h. The resultant solution was centrifuged at 17,000rpm (4°C) for 30min. The supernatant was then loaded onto a Pall centrifugal device (cut-off mass: 1kDa) and centrifuged at 6000rpm for 20min. Deionized water (2mL×3) was added to the membrane, followed by centrifugation at 6000rpm for 20min to remove impurities. The remaining C-dots on the membrane were then collected and lyophilized.

TSBY agar was prepared by dissolving TSB (12g), yeast extract (2g), and agarose (7.4g) in deionized water (400mL). S. aureus and E. coli were cultured on TSBY agar plate at 37°C for 14h. After 14h, a well-separated bacterial colony was carefully lifted from the agar plate and suspended in ammonium acetate buffer (pH 6.5, 25mM, 0.2mL), followed by gentle vortex-mixing at room temperature for 10min. The bacterial suspension (1μL) was mixed with C-dot solution (1μL, 0.1mgml−1) in a vial by pipetting the mixture in and out of the tip for 2min. The resultant mixture (∼0.4μL) was placed on a glass slide and dried at ambient conditions. The glass slide, loaded with the bacteria cells, was observed under a microscope with different excitation wavelengths.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Using C-dots as FRET sensing agents for curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin was initially dissolved in methanol and then serially diluted in solvent containing aqueous sodium acetate (pH 4.0, 0.2M) and methanol (1:1, v/v). When FRET analysis was conducted, the as-prepared C-dots (50μgmL−1, 50μL) were vortex-mixed for 30min with the curcumin solution (50μL) at different concentrations (0, 0.78, 1.56, 3.13, 6.25, 12.50, 25, and 50μM) that were prepared from a serial dilution. The resultant samples were analyzed using fluorescence spectroscopy (λex =300nm).

To investigate the selectivity of C-dots towards curcumin, amino acids were spiked to curcumin samples. Aqueous amino acids (1mM) including l-lysine, l-glutamine, l-histidine, and l-cysteine were prepared in deionized water. The amino acid samples (0.1mL) prepared above were mixed and then added to the solvent (0.6mL) consisting of sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2M) and methanol (1:1, v/v). Curcumin samples (50μL) with different concentrations were vortex-mixed with the amino acid mixture (50μL) for 30min. C-dots were added (25μgmL−1, 50μL) to the samples, and the mixtures were vortex-mixed for another 30min. The resultant samples were analyzed by fluorescence spectroscopy (λex =300nm).

A curcumin stock solution (4mM), initially prepared in methanol, was followed by serial dilution with a solvent containing sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2M) and methanol (1:1, v/v) with a factor of 2. The final concentrations of curcumin solutions were 31.25, 62.50, 125, and 250μM. Turmeric powder (1mg) was dissolved in a solvent (1mL) of sodium acetate (pH 4.0, 0.2M) and methanol (1:1, v/v) under sonication for 30min, followed by centrifugation at 17,000rpm (the radius of the rotor: 8.3cm) at 4°C for 30min. The supernatant was filtered through a membrane (pore size: 0.1μm). The filtered turmeric sample (40μL) was spiked with curcumin (10μL) at different concentrations and then vortex-mixed for 30min. The as-prepared C-dots (10μL, 0.4mgmL−1) were added to the mixture and vortex-mixed for an additional 30min. Fluorescence spectroscopy was conducted to analyze the resultant samples.

In addition, turmeric tablets with a known weight percentage (68%) of curcumin were also selected as the real samples. Three condensed turmeric tablets were weighed and then crushed with a mortar and a pestle. The resultant powder (10mg) was dissolved in a solvent (1mL) containing sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2M) and methanol (1:1, v/v) followed by sonication for 30min. The resultant solution was centrifuged at 17,000rpm (radius of the rotor: 8.3cm) at 4°C for 30min. The supernatant was filtered through a membrane (pore size: 0.1μm) using a syringe. The filtered turmeric tablet extract was 1000-fold diluted by the solvent containing sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2M) and methanol (1:1, v/v). Standard curcumin solution with a known concentration (3.91, 7.81, 15.63, 31.25, 62.50, and 125μM) was prepared in the solvent containing sodium acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 0.2M) and methanol (1:1, v/v). The diluted turmeric tablet extract (40μL) obtained above was spiked with these standard curcumin solutions with different concentration (10μL). After vortex-mixing for 30min, these samples were added with the as-prepared C-dots (10μL, 0.4mgmL−1) under vortex-mixing for another 30min. The resultant samples were examined by fluorescence spectroscopy.

We initially generated C-dots using chicken egg whites as the starting material via a heating method. That is, aqueous egg white solution was heated at 200°C on a hot plate for 4h. After removing large particles, the resultant solution containing C-dots was examined by fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig.1 A shows the fluorescence spectrum of the generated C-dots. The maximum emission band appeared at a wavelength of ∼395nm (λex =300nm). Under illumination of UV light (λmax =365nm), the C-dots emitted blue fluorescence (right vial in the inset in Fig.1A) and had pale brown color 1 For interpretation of color in Figs. 1, 4 and 5, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.
1 observed under room light (left vial in the inset in Fig.1A). No apparent absorption band was observed in the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig.1B). The inset in Fig.1B shows the TEM image of the C-dots. The C-dots appeared quite uniform and had spherical shapes. The average particle size of the C-dots was estimated to be 3.3±0.4nm. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of the as-prepared C-dots was estimated to be ∼6.5ns (Fig. S1), and the QY was estimated to be ∼43% when pyrene was used as the reference standard (QY=32%) [46] (Fig. S2 and Table S1). Fig.1C shows the fluorescence spectra of the C-dots obtained under different excitation wavelengths. The maximum emission wavelength was shifted to a longer wavelength as the excitation wavelength was increased from 300nm to 475nm, which is a typical fluorescence feature of C-dots [2]. The intensity of the fluorescence spectra was decreased when the excitation wavelength was increased to >375nm. Although the mechanism of multicolor emission of C-dots remains under debate, the multicolor emission is presumably due to the contribution of different particle sizes of generated C-dots or resulting from considerable emissive traps present on C-dots [28,47,48]. The results demonstrate that bright C-dots with a high QY can be obtained using egg whites as the starting materials through a simple heating step.

The functionalities on the surface of C-dots were also investigated by FTIR absorption spectroscopy. Fig.2 A shows the FTIR absorption spectrum of the as-prepared C-dots. C-dots were lyophilized to remove water prior to analysis. The absorption band that appeared at 3449cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration mode of OH and NH [49,50], while the band that appeared at 2960cm−1 indicated the presence of the CH stretching vibration mode [51]. In addition, the bands that appeared at 1655cm−1 corresponded to the CO [52] and NH [53] stretching vibration modes. The band at 1404cm−1 indicated the presence of CN [54] stretching vibration mode, and the band that appeared at 1117cm−1 corresponded to the bending vibration mode of CO [55]. These results indicate the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared C-dots. To further confirm the graphite structure of the as-prepared C-dots, we analyzed the C-dots using Raman spectroscopy. Fig.2B shows the Raman spectrum of the generated C-dots. The bands at 1343 and 1542cm−1 dominated the spectrum, indicating the presence of sp3 and sp2 hybrid carbons that corresponded to D and G bands [7,56], respectively. The ratio of the intensity of the D band to the G band was 0.8, indicating a partial disorder in nano-graphite structure that appeared in the generated C-dots.

XPS was also used to characterize the as-generated C-dots. Fig.3 A shows the XPS spectrum of the C-dots. The peaks represent the carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen in the spectrum. Fig.3B shows the XPS spectrum of C1s obtained from the C-dots. The bands that appeared at 286.2eV corresponded to the presence of CO and CN groups [57], while the band at 289.5eV corresponded to carboxylic and carboxylate groups [58]. Fig.3C and D shows the XPS spectra of N1s and O1s obtained from the C-dots, respectively. The band that appeared at 401.1eV (Fig.3C) indicated the presence of protonated amine (NH) [59], whereas the band that appeared at 532.6eV indicated the presence of CO on the C-dots (Fig.3D) [60]. That is, the XPS results also confirmed the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen-containing functional groups on the generated C-dots. The presence of these functional groups also explained the good water solubility of the generated C-dots. Furthermore, the ζ potential of the C-dots was measured to be ∼−13.6mV at pH 7. This result is probably due to the presence of the oxygen-rich functional groups. In addition, the yield of C-dots was estimated to be ∼1.2%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Using C-dots as bacterial labeling agents
ce:section.ce:para: Taking advantage of the high QY, the generated C-dots were further demonstrated to be suitable labeling agents for bacterial cells. E. coli and S. aureus were used as the model bacteria. Fig.4 A shows the optical microscopic images of C-dots investigated under a bright field. Fig.4B–D shows the fluorescence microscopic images obtained by using light at 330–380nm, 450–490nm, and 510–560nm as the excitation wavelengths, respectively. It is appealing that C-dots showed blue, green, and red fluorescence under different excitation light conditions. Fig.4E shows the microscopic image of S. aureus alone under the bright field. S. aureus showed its blue autofluorescence under excitation wavelength at 330–380nm (Fig.4F). However, no fluorescence was emitted from S. aureus when the excitation wavelengths were set at 450–490nm (Fig.4G) and 510–560nm (Fig.4H). After S. aureus cells were labeled with C-dots, the optical microscopic image of the C-dot labeled S. aureus looked similar to that shown in Fig.4E. Nevertheless, bright fluorescence images of the C-dot-labeled S. aureus can be observed under a fluorescence microscope when different excitation light conditions are applied (Fig.4J–L). That is, S. aureus can be readily labeled by C-dots, and their fluorescence microscopic images can be clearly observed. In addition, our C-dots can also be used to label Gram-negative bacteria, such as E. coli. Fig.4M shows the optical microscopic image of E. coli cells observed under a bright field, whereas Fig.4N shows the fluorescence microscopic image of E. coli under excitation wavelengths of 330–380nm. Similar to S. aureus, E. coli can also emit blue autofluorescence (Fig.4N). However, no fluorescence was emitted from E. coli when the excitation wavelengths were set at 450–490nm (Fig.4O) and 510–560nm (Fig.4P). After E. coli cells were labeled with our C-dots, the optical microscopic image of the sample (Fig.4Q) looked similar to that shown in Fig.4M. The C-dot-labeled E. coli emitted different fluorescence (Fig.4R–T) under different excitation light. These results indicate that the generated C-dots are suitable labeling agents for S. aureus and E. coli. Because of the high QY of the generated C-dots, a low concentration (0.1mgmL−1) of C-dots was sufficient to conduct bacterial labeling. Previously, the concentration of C-dots was 2 orders of magnitude [61] higher than the concentration we use herein.

Curcumin is a major component in turmeric products [62] and has been recognized as a potential anti-cancer drug [63]. The quality of turmeric products is usually evaluated by the amount of curcumin they contain. C-dots have been demonstrated to have FRET effect toward curcumin [64]. Given the high QY of C-dots, we believed that our C-dots can be used as more sensitive sensing probes for curcumin than those generated from other methods. Thus, we initially investigated whether the as-prepared C-dots can behave as FRET donors for curcumin. Fig. 5 shows the fluorescence spectrum of the C-dots (red curve) and the absorption spectrum of curcumin (black curve). The inset shows the chemical structure of curcumin. The maximum emission band of the C-dots appeared at the wavelength of 395nm, whereas the maximum absorption band of curcumin appeared at the wavelength of 430nm. There was an overlap between 350nm and 473nm, arising from the fluorescence spectrum of the C-dots and the absorption band of curcumin. Thus, we anticipated that an efficient FRET process might take place.

We further examined whether the C-dots could be used as sensing probes for curcumin. Fig.6 A shows the fluorescence spectra of the samples that contained a given amount of the as-prepared C-dots (50μL, 50μgml−1) with different concentrations of curcumin (50μL). Apparently, the maximum fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 395nm of the C-dots decreased with a significant blue shift from 395nm to 384nm, whereas the fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 545nm of the band representing curcumin increased with increasing curcumin concentration. No difference was observed in the fluorescence intensity and emission center of curcumin with or without the addition of C-dots (Fig. S3). The results show that fluorescence quenching of C-dots is completely attributed to the non-radiative energy transfer to curcumin, and the fluorescence property of curcumin was unchanged during the sensing process. The fluorescence quench and blue shift observed in the fluorescence spectra of C-dots were probably due to the strong interactions between C-dots and curcumin. One of the interactions may result from the formation of hydrogen bonds between C-dots and curcumin. Hydroxyl, carboxylate, and amino groups are present on the surface of C-dots (cf. Figs. 2 and 3), and hydroxyl, methoxy, and keto groups are present in the structure of curcumin. Hydroxyl and amino groups can act as either hydrogen bond donors or acceptors, while carboxylate, methoxy, and keto groups can act as hydrogen bond acceptors. Thus, hydrogen bonds between C-dots and curcumin can readily form to shorten the spatial distance.

In addition, we further examined the excitation spectra of curcumin with and without the presence of C-dots (Fig. S4A) by setting the emission wavelength at 545nm. The excitation spectrum of curcumin in the absence of C-dots (black line) was almost overlapped with the spectrum obtained in the presence of C-dots (red line). The results indicated that the excitation wavelength of curcumin was not influenced in the presence of C-dots. Fig. S4B shows the fluorescence spectra of C-dots (red line), curcumin (black line), and the mixture of C-dots and curcumin (blue line). The fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 395nm decreased in the presence of curcumin (blue line), while the fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 545nm was not changed with or without the presence of C-dots. The results suggested that the decrease of the fluorescence intensity at 395nm is owing to FRET quenching, resulting from non-radiative energy transfer between C-dots and curcumin.

Another fundamental indication used to study the FRET between molecules is to examine the change of fluorescence lifetime of the donor. That is, the fluorescence intensity and the fluorescence lifetime of the donors are decreased when FRET takes place. The fluorescence lifetime of the generated C-dots was examined (Fig. S5) in the absence (red curve) and presence (blue curve) of curcumin (50μM) prepared in the solvent containing sodium acetate (pH 4.0, 0.2M) and methanol (1:1, v/v). The fluorescence lifetime of C-dots was ∼7.7ns because fluorescence lifetime of quantum dots increases significantly in methanol rather than water [65]. However, in the presence of curcumin (25μM), the lifetime of C-dots was decreased to ∼6.1ns. The decrease in the lifetime of C-dots confirmed the efficient energy transfer to the acceptor, leading to strong interaction between C-dots and curcumin. Moreover, the distance between the C-dots and curcumin is in the favorable FRET range. Fig.6B shows the corresponding calibration curve by plotting the fluorescence quenching at the wavelength of 395nm versus the curcumin concentration (where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots in the absence of curcumin, and F is the intensity in the presence of curcumin at the wavelength of 395nm). Good linearity (y=0.024x+0.9555, R 2 =0.997) was obtained in the concentration range of 0.78–50μM, which is wider than that reported previously (2–14μM) [43]. The standard deviation of each data point was <1%. The limit of detection (LOD) of curcumin was determined based on the curve obtained in Fig.6B by using the following formula: LOD=3×(SD)Blank/S. SD is the standard deviation of background, and S is the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD of curcumin was estimated to be ∼40nM (14ngmL−1). Our LOD was much lower than that obtained using conventional methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography as the detection method (∼305ngmL−1) [66]. Furthermore, the LOD for detecting curcumin using C-dots as the sensing probes was 44.8ngmL−1 [64]. That is, our C-dots-based sensing method for the amount of curcumin possessed has a much wider linear range and a lower LOD than previous studies [43,64,66]. The reason may be owing to the high QY of the generated C-dots.

In addition, the energy transfer efficiency or the quenching efficiency of curcumin from the steady-state measurement (Fig.6A) was estimated based on Eq. (1) [67]:
(1)
E
FRET
= ( 1 -
I
DA
/
I
D
) × 100 %
where IDA and ID are the fluorescence intensity of the donor in the presence and absence of the acceptor fluorophore, respectively. In this case, the donor was the C-dots, and the acceptor was curcumin. On the basis of the results from Fig.6A and Eq. (1), we estimated that the energy transfer efficiency was only ∼0.27% in the sample that contained 0.78μM of curcumin, while it was increased to ∼54.73% when the concentration of curcumin in the sample reached 50μM (Table S2). The efficiency of energy transfer was increased with the increasing ratio of acceptor/donor.

Fluorescence lifetime in the presence of an acceptor can be used to determine the FRET efficiency by using Eq. (2) [68]:
(2)
E
FRET
= ( 1 -
τ
DA
/
τ
D
) × 100 %
where τDA and τD are the fluorescence lifetime of the donor, i.e. C-dots, with and without the presence of the acceptor, i.e. curcumin, respectively.

Eq. (2) [68] was used to determine the FRET efficiency based on fluorescence lifetime. τDA and τD were measured to be ∼6.1ns and ∼7.7ns, respectively. Thus, the FRET efficiency was ∼20.78%. When using the spectral overlap method and Eq. (1) to estimate the FRET efficiency [67], the efficiency was ∼33.59% in the presence of curcumin (25μM) (Table S2). The aforementioned results indicate that the energy transfer efficiency and distance measurement depends on experimental conditions [69]. Although the FRET efficiency results obtained from different methods were slightly different, these results suggest that the as-prepared C-dots can be used as the FRET sensing probes for curcumin.

We further examined the selectivity of C-dots toward curcumin in the presence of environmental and biologically relevant molecules. Amino acids, including glutamine, histidine, cysteine, and lysine, were spiked to curcumin samples. The as-prepared C-dots could still be used as FRET sensors for curcumin (Fig. S6). The fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 395nm derived from the C-dots decreased with increasing curcumin concentration in the samples. The trend was similar to when these impurities were absent in the samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of curcumin in turmeric products
ce:section.ce:para: To examine applications of the sensing method to real samples, we further employed the C-dots as sensing probes to determine curcumin concentrations from commercially available turmeric powder. The standard addition method was performed. Fig.7 A shows the fluorescence spectra obtained by mixing the C-dots (10μL, 0.4mgmL−1) and turmeric samples (40μL, 1mgmL−1) that were added with additional curcumin (10μL) with different concentrations. Apparently, the fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 539nm increased with increasing curcumin concentration in the samples. The maximum emission band had a blue shift of 6nm from 545nm to 539nm, compared with the maximum emission band of the curcumin standard. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 395nm derived from the C-dots decreased in tandem with the blue shift as the concentration of the spiked curcumin in turmeric samples increased. The FRET effect of the C-dots toward the turmeric samples remained constant. Fig.7B shows the corresponding calibration curve by plotting the fluorescence intensity change at the wavelength of 395nm derived from the C-dots versus the concentration of curcumin. The calibration curve shows good linearity (y=0.0240x+1.138, R 2 =0.999). The standard deviation of each data point was <∼5%. The estimated curcumin concentration in turmeric powder solution was 47.4μM (∼17.5mg L−1). Because the sample volume of turmeric powder was 40μL, the total amount of curcumin in the sample should be 0.7μg. The original weight of the turmeric powder (40μL×1mgmL−1) in the sample was 40μg. Thus, the concentration of curcumin in the turmeric powder was estimated to be ∼17.5μgg−1. The concentration of curcumin in turmeric powders generally ranges from ∼6μgg−1 to ∼31μgg−1 [62]. Our estimation for the presence of curcumin in turmeric powder was in the common range. The results indicated that our sensing approach can be used to estimate the quantity of curcumin in complex turmeric powder.

In addition, turmeric tablets containing a known percentage (68%, w/w) of curcumin were also examined using our sensing method. Standard addition method was applied to determine the concentration of curcumin in turmeric tablet extract. Fig. S7A shows the representative resultant fluorescence spectra of the turmeric tablet extract (40μL) spiked with standard curcumin solution (10μL) at different concentrations when using the as-prepared C-dots (0.4mg/mL, 10μL) as the sensing probes. The intensity of the fluorescence at the wavelength of ∼545nm increased with the increased concentration of curcumin in the samples. However, the intensity of the fluorescence at 395nm decreased with the increased concentration of curcumin accompanied with a blue shift. Fig. S7B shows the corresponding calibration curve by plotting the change of the fluorescence intensity at the wavelength of 395nm versus the concentration of spiked curcumin. The inset in Fig. S7B shows the linear range used for determination of curcumin in the turmeric tablet extract sample (y=0.0845x+1.0511, R 2 =0.9800). The intercept on X axis was 12.4390μM. Since the sample was 1000-fold diluted before conducting analysis, the concentration of curcumin in the original extract was 12.4390mM (∼4.6mg/mL). Since 10mg tablet powder was dissolved in 1mL of buffer solution when preparing the extract, the amount of curcumin in the original tablet was 4.6mg per 10mg of tablet powder (46%, w/w). The error between the reported method here and the label’s claim was ∼32%. The error in the result may be due to the poor efficiency of our extraction procedure. Nevertheless, our sensing method should be potentially suitable for semi-quantitative analysis.

In this study, we have demonstrated a cost-effective, simple, and rapid method to generate bright C-dots by using inexpensive egg whites as the starting material through a direct heating approach. Compared with the existing synthesis methods that use thermal approaches to generate C-dots, the reaction time in our approach is relatively short. Given the good water solubility and a high QY, the as-prepared C-dots can be potentially used in applications of biosensing and imaging. In this study, we have also demonstrated the feasibility of using the generated C-dots as fluorescent labeling agents for S. aureus and E. coli. C-dots can be used as FRET donors for quantitative detection of curcumin from complex samples. Since the generated C-dots have a high QY and the preparation steps are simple, we believe that the C-dots generated from inexpensive chicken egg whites using this approach should be potentially useful in applications for sensing and imaging.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan (MOST102-2113-M-009-019-MY3) for the financial support of this research. MMFB thanks NCTU for providing him an international student scholarship. We also thank Dr. Anil Kumar Meher for his help in drawing the graphical abstract.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.04.045.
